speaker: interviewer
text: Emilie Stern.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Millie, what did you think during those six months? Did you know where your parents were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. But I did get a few letters that were smuggled to us. And just within the last few years, I have gotten those letters from my mother. And I have them here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's-- I keep losing them. I keep hiding them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You keep hiding them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I keep hiding the letters. There are a few letters in there that I wrote to my parents, but more that they wrote to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still afraid to have it found?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so. Because it is invariably that I don't recall from one time to the next where I put them. And they had been hidden in a haystack during-- during the war and were given to my parents after the war. And obviously, they are-- they are very, very precious and very touching. And my parents will not mention names. They're either initials, they'll sign off as 'Aunt and Uncle,' they'll say 'Oma and Opa,' Grandma and Grandpa, 'have gone on vacation,' which to me was a clue that they, too, were in hiding. But no, I did not know where my parents were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your little brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you felt very responsible for Alex.

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely. I was told to look after him. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said that you were reunited with your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was shocking to walk into a farm and-- and see our parents there. I had, I think gained 10 pounds during that half year. I had eyes that were little slits. So I was well fed, well taken care of. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother, Lex, walked up to our mother, stood in front of her, and said in Dutch-- actually, in Dutch dialect by then-- I know I have seen you somewhere before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he didn't know it was his mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he lost the second mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --The lady who hid him on the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whom we always called 'Aunt.'

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But he-- somehow, that moment-- well, there had been so much going on in those few days and so much turmoil. He felt drawn to our parents, but he couldn't place them. It with a foreign environment. And-- but it didn't take very long that he was snuggling up. We all were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he cry a lot during that six-months period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my recollection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had playmates. There was a little girl who was his age. There was a baby boy who was younger. He had some freedom. There was a farm. There were animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had me. So there was some continuity for him, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you were together. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told that in all probability we could stay together with our parents. But we had to stay inside. And we were at the house of my father's former army buddy, army buddy, who had become extremely active in the resistance, who together with a local preacher-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and his name was Bernard Roerink. I should not say 'my father's army buddy,' because he was such a spectacular man. Together with a local preacher by the name of Lym Overdeim, they saved close to 1,200 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's amazing. How did-- what happened? How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They found hiding places for them. They transported them. They networked. They had people that would steal rationing cards. My father did all the administrative work for all of this because he couldn't go out. So there was a lot of activity going on in our little-- little room where we were in hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were nine of us, nine Jewish people; [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three young men who lived in another part. They lived in the stable, in the attic. We lived in the house, in a little room, in hiding. There was another child with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father sat in a very tiny bedroom in front of the window all day, behind the curtains, and sat watch to make sure that should anybody approach, we would go into our hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was-- a hiding-- our hiding place was a false wall in a closet under a staircase. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Some of us slept there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got to share a bed with my parents for two years, which to this day, I still have questions about. I've never asked. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And when there was any kind of danger, we would all crawl in our hiding, and we did have some-- we did have some Germans that came, and they were suspicious, and they searched, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- but never found us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had an opportunity, or you-- you had to jump into your hiding places.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. We were always prepared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, we did have to go in our hiding place. And the family, Roerink, where we were in hiding, was actually-- and this is very common among Dutch farmers-- there were two families. There were the parents and the young-- the oldest son and his family, all living in one house. And they were all truly, truly magnificent. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I vividly remember one search where they-- the German went up the staircase, over our hiding place, and we heard him say, what's that? To Mr. Roerink. And of course, we couldn't breathe. And then we heard his answer. He said, oh, that's an old Bible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- the answer was, well, we know you have Jews. And he says, well, then you know more than I do. I don't have any Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The courage of these people to-- because there were guns with bayonets drawn. To not say, yes, I have Jews right down here, is to me, beyond comprehension. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's-- and you always lived in a state of readiness. We were allowed to play some, but we had a tablecloth on the floor, and it always had to be within the parameter of the tablecloth, that in case there was any problem, we could grab the tablecloth, and everything-- everything was gone. We had drilled, as far as the sleeping [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] situation, was that the bed that had been used, had been slept in, that somebody else-- when we went in hiding, somebody else would jump in, so there was an explanation why the bed was warm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I haven't thought of this for-- for 50 years, I think. This is coming back right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horror stories of what had happened to resistance workers, what was happening to Jews that had been caught, hearing stories that my aunt and uncle had been found or-- by the Germans and had been deported. The torture that those that were caught doing resistance work were put through, the physical torture, the imprisonments that some went through. Then they came out again. It was always very close because it-- it was such a-- such a hub. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And feeling [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I had to be in control of myself at all times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You're no-- I was no longer a child for a long time already, and I don't think I ever became one again. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And somehow, the-- the-- the fear that you would do something that would-- that would endanger everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like being seen by someone by accidentally moving a curtain. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Somebody seeing the quantity of food that was being prepared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat? Did you have enough?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had enough, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was malnutrition because there was a lack of essentials, but we-- we never had hunger. Being on a farm, they had their own cattle. They slaughtered. They-- And I have memories of having to help with the slaughtering of-- of a pig and being the one who had to stand with a bucket to catch the blood because sausage was made from that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this-- for a city girl, a child, this was-- I don't think I ever ate any of the sausage. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And in my nightmares, which are rather frequent, I still hear a squealing pig. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people were good to us. They were-- we were very fortunate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The elderly lady, the-- the grandmother of the family, would come, was a deeply religious woman who never went to church. But she would come every night to us, and she said, I read such a beautiful little prayer. Let's pray it together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not trying to convert us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They were-- they were such [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unbelievable, unbelievable people. And I know that the Dutch population as a whole was not too great, and the-- the loss of Jewish life was tremendous. My experience has really been very positive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I still fly off the handle when somebody else says, yes, but the Dutch were terrible, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to in one lifetime meet the kind of people that I've met is rather remarkable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to do some studying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Which was-- we had no books, which was nearly impossible. I read whatever was available. There were some old magazines, which, not speaking any English, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I picked out a word here and there, but those magazines, after a while, were no longer available. They became toilet paper. And magazine used extremely good paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rough?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very smooth, very smooth. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a supply of books, I guess the equivalent of Harlequin romances, that I, as an 11-year-old, read over and over and over, just to have something to do. I remember birthdays were celebrated somehow. Even if it was just with an apple, it was-- it was recognized. And I remember one year getting three little tubes of water paint, and what a gift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you use them to paint pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I have not saved any of it. I don't recall what I painted. I just remember I got a yellow, a green, and a red tube of paint, and I thought this was-- this was heaven. This was the biggest gift.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with the others in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Good. I was the only girl. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The-- there were-- there was my brother, and there was another young boy, who was, I guess, one or two years younger than I was. There were three adults, my parents, my grandmother was there, my maternal grandmother, and three young men, Jewish young men, plus the sundry other people that were on the run that stopped in. The Dutch students went in hiding after a while. They had some students from the university. They had-- they had railroad workers, because they objected to transporting Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have major details. I just know there was a strike, and railroad workers were picked up. I recall some English or American pilots that had been downed, because we were in the flight path of the planes that went to Germany. And when some plane was downed, we would have-- there would be a pilot there for a day or two, till he could be moved on. So it--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a hot bed of activity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was very taken with this and very involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very involved, very involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much with the resistance. My mother was the one who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helped in the background. She would, when nobody was around, maintain the house for them. She would do the darning. She would take care of us, obviously. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] What else did she do? I don't know. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I wasn't allowed out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans occupied--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans occupied the area, but we were isolated, being on a farm. So you didn't see that many people until the end of the war. Then, as the Germans were retreating, they came in. And as a matter of fact, they took over the whole farm. They settled in the lower part of the house. They were living, and we were upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans were downstairs. You were upstairs. How can that be? They didn't know you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't go upstairs. They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, it was a huge house. And we were a great deal in our hiding place and in our little bedroom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- toward the very end of the war, the Allies started coming in and flying over and shooting and bombing at the Germans that were retreating. And I remember getting very, very panic-stricken. And that was the time where I remember my mother's mouth-- mother's hand in front of my mouth and saying, no, not now. You can't do this. We're almost-- almost done with it. And being absolutely petrified because I didn't-- there was no safety anywhere anymore. There was danger above, there was danger below.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I bet you had to be careful about walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. You-- you couldn't move. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You just-- why they never came upstairs-- it was a very large house. I-- it was-- they were gentleman farmers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I guess there was enough room that they were able to spend the nights there or something. I'm not-- not quite sure on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the Germans live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably only a matter of days. It seemed like an eternity. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] They-- they saw the danger for them getting closer, and they moved on. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So were-- they weren't dying to find Jews at that point. They were escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were concerned for their own safety. They wanted to get away. And there were deserters among them that hid. Even though there wasn't much forest land, there was some dense brush. And some of the younger ones did-- did sneak out and hid, and they were caught afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother ever think about bleaching your brother's hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was so dark-looking, very deep, deep, brown eyes, very dark-complexioned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think he would-- even with bleached hair, he would have stood out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that point, it just never became a question of getting outside. And the first time I was allowed out at night, toward the end of the war, I fainted. I think I started hyperventilating or something or got so nervous and excited that I just-- I just passed out. I remember falling in the snow. And it felt so good to be out, to-- to smell fresh air.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was while the Germans were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was prior to-- well, the Germans were in Holland, but they were not on the farm there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but when you got let outside--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just-- that was prior to that. That was just briefly one night. That was a big treat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been 11 or 12, probably 12, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going on 100.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember when the Germans were leaving and who came in and how did that whole thing happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Canadian Army came in. And we had a radio in our hiding place. And it was always tuned to the BBC. So we were somewhat up to date as to how far the Allies were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was out of the house when the first tank started rolling, and he almost got killed because they were still shooting at each other. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Seeing the Canadians come in, it-- I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I comprehended it, but I didn't quite believe it, that this was really, yes, I think we've survived it. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there was still-- there was-- there was fear. And there was still-- on the day that we were liberated in '45, that evening, we were allowed to come downstairs and be with the whole family. And the door opened, and here was a young German soldier who had deserted, with a machine gun, facing us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And both my father and Bernard Roerink somehow talked him into putting his weapon down and subduing him. And they took him host-- they took him prisoner. A prisoner. And-- but that we didn't get killed that last minute, to me, that's a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he want when he came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He just wanted to be safe. And I think he was so panic-stricken that if it had meant mowing down a room full of people, he would have. And they were able to reason with him. If I remember correctly, I think my father spoke in German to him. It was a-- he was a child, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they, I guess, handed him over to the Allies. I don't know. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the Canadians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came. Some of them settled down on the farm, stayed there at night. One very young soldier, who rode in a tank, took a great liking to my grandmother and was by her side whenever he could, and we didn't understand why at first. And then he said, she reminds me of my mother. And he drew a picture out of his wallet, and indeed, there was a resemblance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he had to move on, when his group moved on, he had a puppy in his tank. And he handed it to my grandmother and said, would you keep this for me? Because it won't survive. It was a little mongrel, which we kept for years, became our pet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I rem--
